title: Various measures of child labor incidence
subtitle: The series World ILO-EPEAP, as well as the series for England and Italy, cover working children aged 10-14. The series for the US covers working children aged 10-13. The series World ILO-IPEC covers children aged 5-17.
caption:
Various measures of child labor incidence

The series World ILO-EPEAP, as well as the series for England and Italy, cover working children aged 10-14. The series for the US covers working children aged 10-13. The series World ILO-IPEC covers children aged 5-17.

This line chart plots various measures of child labor incidence from 1851 to 2012, with the vertical axis representing percentages from 0% to 70%. The data series are distinguished by color and cover different time periods and age groups. The orange line, representing Italy for children aged 10-14, begins at the highest point on the chart, starting above 60% around 1880. It follows a steep downward trajectory, dropping below 50% by 1900, falling to approximately 30% by 1920, and ending below 5% around 1960. The blue line for England, also covering ages 10-14, starts just under 30% in 1851 and declines to roughly 15% by 1910. The brown line for the United States, covering ages 10-13, begins near 12% in 1890 and decreases to below 5% by 1930. A green line representing the World ILO-EPEAP series appears from roughly 1950 to 1990, starting near 28% and falling to roughly 13%. Finally, a purple line for the World ILO-IPEC series, which covers the broader age range of 5-17, is visible from approximately 1990 to 2012, starting near 16% and ending just above 10%. The chart displays distinct segments for each region or global measure rather than a single continuous timeline for all entities.
